content:
At a particular temperature, the magnitude of the rate constant of a reaction is $5 \times 10^{-5}$ and the unit of the pre-exponential factor of the Arrhenius equation for this reaction is $\mathrm{mol} \mathrm{L}^{-1} \mathrm{~min}^{-1}$. Which of the following plots is correct for this reaction?

$\left[\right.$ Note: $[\mathrm{R}]_0$ is the initial concentration and $t_{1 / 2}$ is the half-life of the reaction $]$

For a reaction $R \rightarrow P$ with a rate constant of $3 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$, which one of the following plots is correct? (Given [R] $]_0$ is the initial concentration of $R$ and $[R]$ is the concentration of $R$ at time t)
explanation:
On the basis of the unit of the reaction we can see that the reaction is a zero order reaction.

$$ \begin{aligned} k & =\frac{\text { Rate }}{[A]^x[B]^v} \\ & =\frac{\text { concentration }}{\text { time }} \times \frac{1}{(\text { concentration })^{\prime \prime}} \quad(\text { where }|A|=|B|) \end{aligned} $$

Taking SI units of concentration, mol $\mathrm{L}^{-1}$ and time, s, the units of $k$ for different reaction order are listed in Table 3.3

$$ \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\text { Table 3.3: Units of rate constant } $$

Zero order reaction 0 mol L − 1 s × 1 ( mol L − 1 ) 6 = mol L − 1 s − 1 mol L − 1 s × 1 mol L − 1 6 = mol L − 1 s − 1 (molL^(-1))/((s))xx(1)/(((mol)L^(-1))^(6))=molL^(-1)s^(-1)
First order reaction 1 mol L − 1 s × 1 ( mol L − 1 ) 1 = s − 1 mol L − 1 s × 1 mol L − 1 1 = s − 1 (molL^(-1))/((s))xx(1)/(((mol)L^(-1))^(1))=s^(-1)
Second order reaction 2 mol L − 1 × 1 s × mol − 1 L s − 1 mol L − 1 × 1 s × mol − 1 L s − 1 (molL)/()^(-1)xx(1)/(s)xxmol^(-1)Ls^(-1)
Fig. 3.3: Variation in the concentration vs time plot for a zero order reaction

content: A first-order reaction is found to have a half-life of one second (sec). What will be the time required for $99.9 \%$ completion of the reaction?
explanation:
To determine the time required for 99.9% completion of a first-order reaction with a half-life of 1 second, we can demonstrate that this time is 10 times the reaction's half-life ($ t_{1/2} $).

When a reaction is 99.9% complete, the remaining concentration is only 0.1% of the initial concentration. This can be expressed as $[R]_t = [R]_0 - 0.999[R]_0$.

Using the first-order kinetics equation:

$ k = \frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[R]_0}{[R]} $

To find the time at which the reaction is 99.9% complete:

$ k = \frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[R]_0}{0.001[R]_0} = \frac{2.303}{t} \log 10^3 $

Thus:

$ t = \frac{6.909}{k} $

For the half-life of a first-order reaction:

$ t_{1/2} = \frac{0.693}{k} $

Comparing the times:

$ \frac{t}{t_{1/2}} = \frac{6.909}{k} \times \frac{k}{0.693} = 10 $

Therefore, the time required for 99.9% completion is approximately 10 times the half-life, or 10 seconds. This coincides with option C, 10 seconds.
